scenario: Orion Retailer runs an order fulfillment process on Camunda 8.8 that calls 3 synchronous REST services for payment, inventory, and shipping. The platform team handles 22000 orders per day and is deciding whether to ship one process solution that owns all 3 workers and the BPMN model or to split each worker into its own deployable application from day one.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 best-practices guide recommend as the default packaging for the glue code workers of a single process?

options.text: Bundle all glue code workers and the BPMN model into one process solution application that is versioned, tested, and deployed together as a single self-contained unit
Deploy each job worker as its own standalone microservice from day one, keeping the BPMN model in a separate central modeling repository owned by a platform team
Embed each worker directly inside the calling system itself, so payment runs in the payment service and shipping runs in the shipping service with no shared application
Run all workers inside the Zeebe broker as in-process handlers and keep the BPMN model in a separate deployment pipeline owned by the operations team

a.text: Correct. The guide recommends implementing all glue code in one process solution application that also deploys the BPMN model, producing a self-contained unit that is easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy.

b.text: Incorrect. Splitting every worker into its own service is described only as an exception for scaling, throttling, or polyglot needs, not the default packaging.

c.text: Incorrect. Embedding workers in the called systems is not the recommended pattern; the default is a self-contained process solution holding the glue code.

d.text: Incorrect. Job workers run as external clients, not inside the Zeebe broker; the recommended default is one process solution application owning the workers and model.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 writing-good-workers guide recommends a single process solution application that contains all glue code workers and deploys the BPMN model, with carved-out workers used only when a task needs independent scaling, throttling, or a different language.
